text: The surgeon identifies the lateral pelvic fascia during the nerve sparing dissection, revealing the tissue caliber between the prostate capsule and the neurovascular bundle. Magnification highlights nerve fibers within the web-like tissue structure.

The surgeon retracts the seminal vesicle on the left side, exposing the pedicle, the main blood supply to the posterior prostate. The pedicle is grasped with forceps, and a clip is applied to control bleeding. Additional clips are placed, revealing the beginning and end of the nerve bundle. A sharp incision is made with minimal blood loss, enabling precise dissection along the prostate capsule in a low volume disease scenario. Another clip is applied to facilitate complete dissection of the nerve bundle.

The surgeon applies minimal traction on the prostate, revealing the triangular-shaped nerve bundle sweeping off to the prostate capsule. Under magnification, individual nerve fibers are visible at the millimeter level. A small perforating blood vessel is managed using cautery.

The surgeon performs the right-sided neurovascular dissection, revealing the prostate capsule and base above the scissors. Individual blood vessels in the pedicle are visible, with clips being precisely placed by the assistant under the surgeon's guidance. The nerve dissection is visible, showing the nerve lateral and below the prostate.

The surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to place a clip on the nerve bundle, allowing it to fall laterally and posteriorly. The assistant applies gentle traction to stretch the nerve bundle, aiding in visual identification for precise cutting. The neurovascular bundle, appearing as a tubular structure about a centimeter wide, is visible above the sucker tip and to the right of the dissection area.

The surgeon incises the dorsal vein complex, establishing continuity between the nerve dissection and urethral dissection. The yellow Foley catheter is visible in the midline. The surgeon connects the nerve dissection laterally and apically, using the robotic system for precise manipulation in tight quarters. The posterior urethral plate is cut, followed by dissection through the recto urethralis tissue behind and underneath the prostate, ensuring the neurovascular bundles remain intact.

The prostate is now detached, revealing the neurovascular bundles with clips visible on both the right and left sides. The surgeon uses a vascular Doppler ultrasound to assess the arterial pulse within these bundles, which is crucial for maintaining blood flow to the cavernosal artery.
